Buy the Book
Memoirist  ·  Survivor  ·  Still Here

Christopher
Carazas

Here is what nobody tells you about writing a memoir. The hardest part is not the writing. The hardest part is deciding that your story is worth taking up space in someone else's life. I was not sure mine was. Turns out I was wrong about that.

Read the Memoir Read the Essays →

Is This For You?

This book is not for everyone.
It is for you, specifically, if…

You have been told you are too much, and have spent years becoming less to make other people more comfortable with your existence.
You have functioned flawlessly on the outside while quietly running a five-alarm emergency inside, and called it fine.
You have survived something you were never supposed to talk about, and you are still looking for the right words for it.
You have been diagnosed with something and received, in place of support, a new and more creative form of blame.
You want the truth about what survival actually looks like. Not the version with soft lighting and ten actionable takeaways. The real one.
You love someone who carries things quietly, and you want to understand what that costs them.

If any of those sentences made you exhale, you are already in the right place. Pull up a chair. Shadow has inspected it and found it morally acceptable.

The Memoir

Now That I'm Still Here
A Memoir of Ruin and Resurrection

Here is what I will tell you about this book. It does not have a triumphant chapter twelve. There is no moment where the music swells and everyone learns their lesson. What it has is something more useful: the actual truth about what happens when your life falls apart completely and you have to decide, without any real help from the movies, whether to put it back together.

It covers psychological abuse. Nights when staying felt impossible. A late-in-life autism diagnosis that arrived not as a gift but as an explanation for every difficult year that came before it. These are not light topics. I did not write about them lightly.

There is also a 13-year-old German Shepherd named Shadow. She was present for all of it and has made clear, in her own way, that she does not need a dedication page to know her role in the story. She has a bow tie. She wears it with authority.

This is not a triumph narrative. It is a reckoning. And if you have ever felt too much, masked too well, or wondered whether breath still counts after everything breaks, this book was written for you. Exactly as you are right now.

South Shore Autism Center AFSP South Shore (American Foundation for Suicide Prevention) MEDA (Multi-Service Eating Disorders Association)

Every copy sold funds these three organizations. Your purchase is part of that.

“

I did not write this book because I had the answers. I wrote it because I had finally stopped pretending I did. Somewhere in that surrender, I found the only thing that ever actually helped: the truth, shared honestly, for whoever needs it most.

From the Memoir

About the Author

Christopher
Carazas

I was born in Paraguay and raised across multiple countries on multiple continents. By the time I was an adult, I had called Ecuador, Bolivia, Switzerland, Senegal, Ghana, Madagascar, Oman, Turkey, and the United States home. I worked in international development across Haiti, Mali, Benin, Bangladesh, and beyond. I became fluent in several languages, several cultures, and several very different ideas about what a person was supposed to be.

What that teaches you, when you pay close enough attention, is how to disappear. How to become whatever the room needs. I got very good at it. It turns out that is less of a skill than it sounds, and more of a survival strategy with a very high long-term cost.

Diagnosed autistic as an adult, I spent years functioning by shrinking. Then the marriage ended. The silence moved in. The mask I had been wearing since childhood finally gave out. My memoir tells that story the way it actually happened: in disorder, in grief, in the slow and stubborn work of deciding to stay.

I live in Kingston, Massachusetts now. The ghosts here are quieter. The silence is no longer a weapon. Healing, I have discovered, is not a destination. It is just what it looks like when you keep moving forward even when you are not entirely sure where forward is.

My Substack publishes every week: essays on survival, grief, neurodivergent life, and what rebuilding actually looks like. It also features Shadow's dispatches on household management, which get better engagement than most things I write. I have made peace with this.

Read the Essays

The Heart of the Story

Katie.

She appears in the memoir as Ava. Her name was Katie.

Loving her changed me, and losing her nearly broke me. Writing her into the book was the hardest choice I made. It meant admitting she was gone. But it also meant saying she mattered, that what we had was real, and that I still carry her with me.

This memoir exists because of her. What began as a story written for one person became something written for everyone who has ever needed it. The Road to 2,000 carries her name forward. Every copy sold is a mile walked with her still in it.

Read Her Story Her chapter is called The Girl Who Stayed.
Vineyard, Fall 2024
Blue Hour, Plymouth
The Great Lobster Incident
Key West, Summer 2024
Old Silver, Autumn 2024

Resident Critic & Appointed Guardian

Shadow

German Shepherd. 13 years old. Madagascar-born. Currently wearing a bow tie. Unimpressed, but present.

Shadow did not sign up for any of this. She arrived from Madagascar in 2012 carrying the expression of someone who had already seen everything and was not especially impressed by what she found. She was not adopted. She was assigned. There is a difference. Adoption implies a choice was made. What happened between Shadow and me felt more like a reckoning. She looked at me, exhaled once like she had been waiting, and that was that.

She appears throughout the memoir as a sentinel and guardian. The one heartbeat in the room that never arrived with an agenda. She runs the Plymouth Sentinel and Canine Gazette as Editor-in-Chief, covering local governance, snack allocation, and the ongoing failure of this household to meet her documented standards.

The bow tie is not a costume. The bow tie is a statement. Our institutional review board, which consists entirely of Shadow, has confirmed that it communicates authority. The open mouth communicates opinion. She had both that day and has not stopped since.

Shadow did not consent to this website. She is aware of it. Her editorial notes were withheld on legal advice, and also because they were devastating.

Editor-in-Chief

The Campaign

The Road to 2,000

Turns out you can take a near-death experience and build a mission around it. Here is how it works, and why the math matters more than it might look like at first glance.

For every 2,000 copies of the memoir sold, $6,000 is donated to three organizations doing the quiet, unglamorous, essential work of keeping people alive. That is $2,000 each to South Shore Autism Center, AFSP South Shore (American Foundation for Suicide Prevention), and MEDA (Multi-Service Eating Disorders Association). Then the cycle resets and we do it again.

Campaign Progress 0 of 2,000 copies
0 250 500 1,000 1,500 2,000
2,000
Copies to the goal
$6,000
Donated per cycle
3:1
Est. social return

The Social Return: What $6,000 Actually Does

Social Return on Investment (SROI) asks a simple question: for every dollar donated, how much measurable social value is created? Here is the honest answer for this campaign.

Research on mental health intervention programs consistently shows a return of $2.50 to $5.00 in social value per dollar invested, through reduced emergency healthcare costs, improved employment outcomes, decreased long-term public assistance needs, and the enormous economic impact of lives that stayed. Applied conservatively across the three organizations this campaign supports, every $6,000 donated generates approximately $18,000 in measurable social value.

3:1

Conservative estimated SROI

Every $1 donated through this campaign creates approximately $3 in measurable social value. The narrative value of the book itself, what happens when the right story reaches the right person at the right moment, is harder to quantify and probably larger. The Department of Acceptable Suffering does not have a form for this kind of math. But the math is real.

Milestones Along the Way

250

The Alternate Ending

A deleted ending released to readers. The version of the story that almost was, before the one that actually happened.

500

Live Event

A live gathering with the community. Readings, conversation, and Shadow judging all of us from a safe distance. Her attendance has been confirmed. Her criteria for approval remain classified.

1K

Halfway Home

Proof that one person's survival can become a network of lifelines. The halfway mark gets its own moment, because it deserves one.

1.5K

Grief and Survival Livestream

A collective exhale. A conversation about what staying actually costs, and what it gives back. The kind of room that does not exist enough.

2K

The Full $6,000 Donated. Cycle Two Begins.

$2,000 each to South Shore Autism Center, AFSP South Shore, and MEDA. Then we start again. Hope is not a one-time offer.

Be Part of the Road

Every copy is a vote for the next person who needs this story before they know how to ask for it.

Shadow’s Weekly Dispatch

Every Sunday, Shadow publishes her official report on the state of the household, the harbor, Plymouth governance, and the ongoing failure of the human to meet her standards. The Gazette covers what the mainstream press will not. It maintains that the world is upheld not by the sleek and the strategic, but by smaller offices: witness, loyalty, inspection, refusal, and the maintenance of thresholds.

Sunday Edition
June 14, 1886 (2026)
In which civilization continues to fail quietly, and Shadow files her report from the editorial rug.

From the Writing Desk

The Weekly Writing

Every week: essays on what survival actually looks like, why the wellness industry has the whole thing slightly wrong, and what it means to stay when staying is the hardest thing you have ever done.

Subscribe on Substack →

Essay

The Myth of Being Fixed

We want the survivor showered, articulate, and emotionally available for a podcast by Thursday. We have created an entire economy around acceptable suffering. This essay is about why that system is stupid, and also slightly official.

Essay

You Don't Have to Earn Staying Alive

Nobody tells you this. Survival does not come with a receipt. There is no certificate, no form, no panel of judges. You stayed. That is the whole requirement. That counts.

Satire

A Field Guide to Unhelpful Concern

A scientific taxonomy of people who mean well and miss the point entirely. The Fix-It Evangelist. The Wellness Evangelist. The Check-In Text Artist. Study the specimens carefully.

Essay

Nobody Tells You Survival Sends an Invoice

You get out. You survive. And then, some time later, the invoice arrives. The itemization is brutal and specific. This is the unpaid bill nobody mentioned when they told you to hold on.

Essay

Every Room Has a Password

You can do everything right. Make yourself legible, useful, safe. And still be loved only in translation. Still be welcomed only in excerpt. Some rooms hand you the password and still never let you in.

Essay

The Gospel According to Where Are You From?

People ask it like it's a simple question. Where are you from. The honest answer requires a map, a timeline, and a willingness to explain why home is not a place but a category of loss.

Connect

Come Find Me

I write on Substack every week and appear on podcasts regularly. Shadow is present in an advisory capacity. She prefers not to be tagged, but she monitors all mentions.
